past_medical_history: Recurrent upper and lower respiratory tract infections over the past 2 years, treated with multiple courses of oral antibiotics
Recurrent otitis media, 4 episodes in the past 18 months
Diagnosed with adult-onset asthma 18 months ago at a primary care clinic, treated with bronchodilators and inhaled corticosteroids
Bronchiectasis noted on chest CT 6 months ago, attributed to recurrent infections
Pneumococcal pneumonia 3 months ago requiring hospitalization and IV ceftriaxone, responded to treatment
Received pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine 1 year ago without subsequent reduction in infection frequency
No prior neurological complaints or hospitalizations for neurological conditions

medications.indication: Current course for upper respiratory infection symptoms started 5 days ago

family_history: Mother, age 75, alive, hypertension and osteoarthritis
Father, deceased age 68, lung cancer (former smoker)
No family history of immunodeficiency, autoimmune diseases, or neurological disorders

social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine, 1-2 per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two teenage children
social_history.travel: No recent travel outside the country

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but appears fatigued and mildly irritable. Oriented to person and place, but initially states the date incorrectly by one day. GCS 14 (E4, V4, M6). Attention mildly impaired on serial 7s (4/5 correct). Short-term recall 2/3 objects at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. No facial asymmetry. Mild photophobia reported but does not withdraw to penlight. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. No dysarthria.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. Normal bulk and tone throughout.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick sensation intact and symmetric in all extremities. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin performed normally without dysmetria bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Not formally tested; patient reports mild unsteadiness when standing, attributes it to headache and malaise.
neurological_exam.additional: Mild neck stiffness on passive flexion; Kernig sign equivocal (patient reports posterior thigh tightness but unclear if this represents true meningismus or musculoskeletal discomfort). Brudzinski sign negative. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Mild conjunctival injection bilaterally. Bilateral coarse crackles at lung bases on auscultation. Mild splenomegaly on palpation.

patient.chief_complaint: Worsening headache and neck stiffness for 2 days, with confusion noticed by husband today
patient.history_present_illness:
A 49-year-old woman who works as a childminder presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of progressive headache and neck stiffness, with new confusion noticed by her husband earlier today. She has a well-documented 2-year history of recurrent upper and lower respiratory tract infections, including multiple episodes of otitis media and one hospitalization for pneumococcal pneumonia three months ago. She was previously diagnosed with adult-onset asthma based on wheezing episodes, though her pulmonary function tests reportedly showed no bronchodilator reversibility, and a subsequent workup revealed low exhaled nitric oxide and no eosinophilia, casting doubt on the asthma diagnosis. Bronchiectasis was noted on imaging six months ago. She received pneumococcal vaccination one year ago but continued to develop infections at the same frequency.

Five days prior to presentation, she developed nasal congestion, productive cough, and low-grade fevers, for which her primary care physician prescribed amoxicillin-clavulanate empirically. Over the subsequent three days, she noted progressive frontal headache initially attributed to sinusitis. Two days ago, she developed posterior neck stiffness which she attributed to sleeping in an awkward position. She took acetaminophen with partial relief. Yesterday, her husband noted she seemed more confused than usual, had difficulty following conversations, and was slower in her responses. She reported mild nausea without vomiting and subjective fevers at home, though her measured temperature was only 37.4 degrees Celsius with acetaminophen on board. She denies seizures, focal weakness, visual changes, or rash. She had no recent travel, no sick contacts beyond the children at her daycare, and no known exposure to tuberculosis. She denies any prior neurological symptoms. She presents to the ED tonight after her husband became concerned about her increasing lethargy and confusion.

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Equivocal thin linear enhancement along sulci, may represent normal vascular enhancement versus early leptomeningeal process

signal_characteristics.T2: High signal within sinuses consistent with mucosal edema and possible retained secretions

additional_observations: Paranasal sinus disease as described, clinically correlate for sinusitis
Normal brain parenchymal volume for age
mri.impression: Equivocal subtle leptomeningeal enhancement along the bilateral frontoparietal convexities, which may represent normal vascular enhancement or early meningeal process. Clinical correlation is recommended. Significant paranasal sinus disease consistent with acute-on-chronic sinusitis. No evidence of abscess, empyema, or parenchymal lesion.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low — no LP performed prior to imaging

recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with CSF analysis if meningitis is suspected
Consider repeat MRI with contrast if clinical picture evolves
ENT referral for paranasal sinus disease if not already evaluated

ecg.interpretation: Normal ECG. Sinus tachycardia borderline at 94 bpm, likely related to febrile illness or anxiety.
ecg.clinical_correlation: No acute cardiac pathology. Heart rate mildly elevated, consistent with infection or systemic inflammatory state.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mild leukocytosis; less pronounced than expected for severe bacterial infection, possibly blunted by partial antibiotic treatment or underlying immunodeficiency
Neutrophilic predominance consistent with bacterial infection
Lymphopenia; may reflect acute stress response or underlying immune dysregulation
Left shift suggesting active bacterial infection

BMP.clinical_significance: Hyponatremia, consider SIADH in setting of CNS infection
Slightly low, may reflect mild metabolic acidosis

Inflammatory_markers.clinical_significance: Elevated but not markedly so; partially treated bacterial infection may blunt the inflammatory response
Elevated, suggestive of bacterial infection but below the >2 threshold often seen in severe sepsis
Elevated, consistent with inflammatory or infectious process

LFT.clinical_significance: Low total protein, may indicate protein loss or decreased production — consider further evaluation
Mild hypoalbuminemia, can be seen in chronic illness or inflammation

labs.interpretation: Mild leukocytosis with neutrophilic predominance and bandemia, consistent with bacterial infection. Inflammatory markers (CRP, procalcitonin, ESR) are elevated but not as markedly as expected for fulminant bacterial meningitis — partial antibiotic treatment with amoxicillin-clavulanate may be blunting the systemic inflammatory response. Hyponatremia raises concern for SIADH, which can occur with CNS infections. Low total protein and hypoalbuminemia are notable and could indicate underlying humoral immune deficiency or chronic illness. Stress hyperglycemia present. No evidence of DIC. Urinalysis negative for UTI.

csf.glucose_ratio: 0.35 (serum glucose 108 mg/dL; ratio borderline low, reference >0.6)
csf.interpretation: Mixed pleocytosis with nearly equal proportions of neutrophils and lymphocytes. Mildly elevated protein and borderline low glucose with a CSF-to-serum glucose ratio of 0.35 (below normal threshold of 0.6). Clear appearance without xanthochromia argues against subarachnoid hemorrhage. Gram stain is negative, which may reflect low bacterial burden, early presentation, or partial treatment with oral antibiotics. Elevated CSF lactate (3.8 mmol/L) favors bacterial over viral etiology. The overall CSF profile is ambiguous — findings are compatible with early or partially treated bacterial meningitis, but viral meningitis or other inflammatory etiologies cannot be excluded based on initial CSF alone. CSF culture and additional molecular testing recommended.

special_tests.Gram_stain: Gram-positive diplococci identified (morphology consistent with Streptococcus pneumoniae)
special_tests.CSF_culture: Growth at 24 hours: Streptococcus pneumoniae, susceptibility testing pending

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture at 48 hours shows significant progression: marked neutrophilic pleocytosis (320 WBC, 82% neutrophils), markedly elevated protein, and critically low glucose ratio (0.21). Gram stain now reveals gram-positive diplococci and culture is growing Streptococcus pneumoniae. CSF lactate is markedly elevated at 6.2 mmol/L. These findings definitively confirm acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae. The initial partially treated presentation with negative gram stain has now evolved to a classic bacterial CSF profile. Antibiotic intensification and continued monitoring recommended.

Blood_cultures.clinical_significance: Early growth in aerobic bottle raises high suspicion for clinically significant bacteremia
Pneumococcal bacteremia confirms hematogenous spread; source likely meningitis
Intermediate penicillin susceptibility; high-dose IV penicillin or ceftriaxone should be effective for meningeal dosing
Confirms ceftriaxone is appropriate empiric and targeted therapy
Vancomycin susceptible; can be used in combination per guidelines

output.interpretation: Blood cultures positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae with early growth (18 hours). Organism shows intermediate penicillin susceptibility but is fully susceptible to ceftriaxone and vancomycin. This confirms pneumococcal bacteremia with likely meningeal source. The partially treated state with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate was insufficient to clear the infection. Given the patient's history of recurrent pneumococcal infections despite vaccination, underlying humoral immunodeficiency (e.g., common variable immunodeficiency) should be investigated after acute treatment.
abnormal_values_summary: Blood cultures: Streptococcus pneumoniae (positive at 18 hours)

CSF_meningitis_encephalitis_panel.clinical_significance: Confirms pneumococcal meningitis even with initial negative gram stain

output.interpretation: CSF multiplex PCR panel (FilmArray) is positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae, confirming pneumococcal meningitis. All other bacterial, viral, and fungal targets are negative. This result is particularly valuable given the initially negative gram stain, likely due to partial oral antibiotic treatment prior to presentation.

Immunoglobulins.clinical_significance: Markedly decreased IgG, highly suggestive of common variable immunodeficiency (CVID)
Markedly decreased IgA, consistent with CVID
Low IgE argues against allergic/atopic etiology for recurrent wheezing

Vaccine_responses.value: Non-protective titers to 12 of 14 serotypes tested
Vaccine_responses.reference_range: Protective titers (>1.3 mcg/mL) to majority of serotypes
Vaccine_responses.clinical_significance: Failed vaccine response despite pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccination 1 year ago, diagnostic for impaired humoral immunity

HIV_screen.clinical_significance: Rules out HIV as cause of secondary immunodeficiency

output.interpretation: Panhypogammaglobulinemia with markedly reduced IgG (185 mg/dL), IgA (12 mg/dL), and IgM (28 mg/dL). Failed pneumococcal vaccine response with non-protective titers to most serotypes. HIV screening negative. These findings are diagnostic of Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID) and explain the patient's 2-year history of recurrent sinopulmonary infections, bronchiectasis, failed vaccine response, and now invasive pneumococcal disease (meningitis). CVID predisposes to encapsulated organism infections, particularly Streptococcus pneumoniae. Immunology referral for IVIG initiation is strongly recommended after acute meningitis treatment.

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
output.impression: Non-contrast CT head demonstrates no acute intracranial abnormality. No contraindication to lumbar puncture. Incidental paranasal sinus disease consistent with sinusitis.
recommended_actions: Lumbar puncture may proceed if clinically indicated
MRI with contrast for further evaluation if meningitis is suspected clinically

output.query: partially treated bacterial meningitis CSF findings diagnosis

results.title: Effect of Partial Treatment on CSF Findings in Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: Nigrovic LE, Malley R, et al.
results.summary: Prior antibiotic use can sterilize CSF cultures in up to 44% of cases and reduce CSF WBC counts. CSF glucose and protein are less affected by partial treatment. Gram stain sensitivity drops from 80% to less than 40% with prior antibiotics.

results.title: CSF Lactate as a Diagnostic Marker in Partially Treated Meningitis
results.authors: Sakushima K, Hayashino Y, et al.
results.summary: CSF lactate >3.5 mmol/L has sensitivity of 93% and specificity of 96% for bacterial meningitis, and is less affected by prior antibiotic use than culture or gram stain. Recommended as adjunctive test when culture-negative meningitis is suspected.

results.title: Mixed Pleocytosis in Bacterial Meningitis: Frequency and Diagnostic Implications
results.authors: Brouwer MC, Thwaites GE, et al.
results.summary: Up to 10% of confirmed bacterial meningitis cases present with lymphocytic predominance, particularly in partially treated patients or in early disease. Mixed pleocytosis should not exclude bacterial meningitis from the differential.

output.summary: Prior oral antibiotic treatment can significantly alter CSF findings in bacterial meningitis: gram stain sensitivity drops below 40%, cultures may be sterilized, and the cellular response may shift toward lymphocytic or mixed pleocytosis. CSF lactate (>3.5 mmol/L) and CSF-to-serum glucose ratio (<0.4) remain reliable discriminators even after partial treatment. Multiplex PCR panels can detect bacterial DNA even when cultures are negative.

output.proposed: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h + Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h
interactions: Dexamethasone may reduce vancomycin CNS penetration by reducing meningeal inflammation; monitor clinical response closely
Ceftriaxone and vancomycin: no direct pharmacokinetic interaction but nephrotoxicity risk should be monitored
contraindications: Patient has sulfonamide allergy — trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole should be avoided if needed empirically
warnings: Dexamethasone should ideally be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics for maximal benefit in pneumococcal meningitis
If dexamethasone is started, full 4-day course (0.15 mg/kg q6h) is recommended per IDSA guidelines
Monitor renal function with vancomycin; target trough or AUC/MIC per institutional protocol
output.formulary_status: All three medications are formulary and recommended per IDSA bacterial meningitis guidelines
alternatives: If ceftriaxone-resistant pneumococcus: consider meropenem 2g IV q8h
If vancomycin intolerance: consider linezolid 600 mg IV q12h (less CNS data)
Ampicillin 2g IV q4h should be added if Listeria coverage is needed (age >50 or immunocompromised)

Audiometry.clinical_significance: Mild sensorineural hearing loss in the right ear, likely complication of pneumococcal meningitis
Normal hearing in the left ear
Reduced speech discrimination consistent with sensorineural hearing loss

output.interpretation: Audiometry reveals mild right-sided sensorineural hearing loss (PTA 35 dB HL) with reduced speech discrimination (84%), consistent with a known complication of pneumococcal meningitis (cochlear damage from inflammatory process). Left ear hearing is within normal limits. Follow-up audiometry recommended in 4-6 weeks to assess for recovery or progression.
abnormal_values_summary: Right ear PTA 35 dB HL (mild sensorineural hearing loss)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae, in the setting of undiagnosed Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID)

differential.likelihood: Moderate initially — mixed pleocytosis and clear CSF mimic viral etiology, but low glucose ratio and elevated CSF lactate argue against it

differential.diagnosis: Viral upper respiratory infection with tension headache
differential.likelihood: Moderate initially — recent URI symptoms and low-grade fever could represent uncomplicated viral illness, but progressive confusion and neck stiffness warrant further workup

differential.diagnosis: Acute sinusitis with secondary intracranial extension
differential.likelihood: Low-to-moderate — paranasal sinus disease on imaging and URI symptoms could suggest complicated sinusitis, but CSF profile is more consistent with hematogenous meningitis
Low — subacute presentation and lymphocytic CSF could raise concern, but no risk factors, no basilar enhancement, and relatively acute course argue against TB
Low — CSF cryptococcal antigen negative; patient is not classically immunocompromised (no HIV, no transplant), though underlying CVID could theoretically predispose

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT head to rule out mass lesion or elevated ICP before lumbar puncture
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No acute intracranial abnormality, safe to proceed with LP

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture, and lactate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mixed pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein, borderline low glucose, negative gram stain (partially treated), elevated lactate

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including CBC, BMP, CRP, procalcitonin, and coagulation studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild leukocytosis with left shift, elevated inflammatory markers, hyponatremia (SIADH), low total protein

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) and adjunctive dexamethasone immediately, do not wait for CSF results
Order CSF meningitis/encephalitis multiplex PCR panel to identify pathogen given negative gram stain
Draw blood cultures before starting IV antibiotics
Obtain brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for meningeal enhancement and complications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Equivocal leptomeningeal enhancement, paranasal sinus disease

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on partially treated meningitis CSF findings to guide interpretation of ambiguous initial CSF
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prior antibiotics can sterilize cultures and alter CSF profile; CSF lactate remains a reliable marker

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for empiric meningitis regimen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Dexamethasone may reduce vancomycin CNS penetration; sulfonamide allergy noted

optimal_actions.action: Investigate underlying immunodeficiency given recurrent infections: order immunoglobulin levels, vaccine response titers, and HIV screening
Perform repeat lumbar puncture at 48 hours to assess treatment response
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Worsening CSF profile initially but culture confirms S. pneumoniae

optimal_actions.action: Order audiometry to screen for sensorineural hearing loss as complication of pneumococcal meningitis

critical_actions: Start empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) immediately without waiting for CSF results given clinical suspicion for bacterial meningitis
Administer adjunctive dexamethasone before or with the first dose of antibiotics
Perform lumbar puncture to obtain CSF for analysis
Order CSF multiplex PCR panel given negative gram stain in the context of prior antibiotic use
Recognize that the mixed pleocytosis with elevated CSF lactate and low glucose ratio suggests bacterial meningitis despite negative gram stain and clear CSF
Identify the pattern of recurrent encapsulated organism infections and failed vaccine response as suggestive of underlying humoral immunodeficiency
contraindicated_actions: Discharging patient with diagnosis of viral meningitis or URI without adequate workup — the elevated CSF lactate and low glucose ratio mandate empiric bacterial coverage
Delaying antibiotics to obtain CSF or additional testing — time to antibiotics is a critical determinant of outcome in bacterial meningitis
Performing LP without prior CT in a patient with altered mental status — risk of herniation must be excluded
Prescribing oral antibiotics alone for suspected meningitis — IV antibiotics with adequate CNS penetration are required
Omitting dexamethasone in suspected pneumococcal meningitis — adjunctive steroids reduce mortality and hearing loss
Administering trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole — patient has documented sulfonamide allergy
key_reasoning_points: The clear CSF with mixed pleocytosis and negative gram stain is misleading — prior oral antibiotic treatment (amoxicillin-clavulanate for 5 days) has partially treated the meningitis, reducing WBC counts, sterilizing initial gram stain/culture, and shifting the differential toward viral etiology
Elevated CSF lactate (3.8 mmol/L, >3.5 threshold) is the single most useful discriminator between bacterial and viral meningitis and is less affected by partial antibiotic treatment
The CSF-to-serum glucose ratio of 0.35 (<0.4 normal threshold) is a strong indicator of bacterial etiology despite the borderline absolute glucose value of 38 mg/dL

The patient's 2-year history of recurrent sinopulmonary infections, bronchiectasis, otitis media, pneumococcal pneumonia, and failure to respond to pneumococcal vaccination constitutes a classic presentation of Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID)
Low total serum protein (5.2 g/dL) and hypoalbuminemia on initial labs are red flags for hypogammaglobulinemia, providing an early clue to the underlying immunodeficiency

The original 'asthma' diagnosis was incorrect — lack of bronchodilator reversibility, low exhaled nitric oxide, and absence of eosinophilia excluded true asthma; the wheezing was due to bronchiectasis from recurrent infections
CVID predisposes to infections with encapsulated organisms (particularly S. pneumoniae), explaining the progression from recurrent pneumonia to invasive meningitis
Hyponatremia (sodium 131) in the context of CNS infection should raise concern for SIADH, a common complication of bacterial meningitis

metadata.condition_name: Acute bacterial meningitis (Streptococcus pneumoniae) with underlying Common Variable Immunodeficiency
metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle: presentation is masked by 5 days of prior oral antibiotic treatment, producing clear CSF with mixed pleocytosis and negative gram stain that initially mimics viral meningitis. The patient is afebrile in the ED (on acetaminophen), has only mild neck stiffness with equivocal meningeal signs, and prominent sinusitis on imaging provides an alternative explanation for symptoms. The critical clues — elevated CSF lactate, low glucose ratio, and the clinical pattern of recurrent encapsulated organism infections pointing to CVID — require careful integration across multiple data sources.
